Here are pytest‑style unit tests (plain text, no special formatting):

import pytest
from your_module import parse_csv_line

def test_simple_csv():
    line = "a,b,c"
    assert parse_csv_line(line) == ["a", "b", "c"]

def test_quoted_field():
    line = '"a,b",c'
    assert parse_csv_line(line) == ["a,b", "c"]

def test_multiple_quoted_fields():
    line = '"x,y","p,q","r"'
    assert parse_csv_line(line) == ["x,y", "p,q", "r"]

def test_empty_fields():
    line = "a,,c"
    assert parse_csv_line(line) == ["a", "", "c"]

def test_trailing_empty_field():
    line = "a,b,"
    assert parse_csv_line(line) == ["a", "b", ""]

def test_leading_empty_field():
    line = ",b,c"
    assert parse_csv_line(line) == ["", "b", "c"]

def test_all_empty():
    line = ",,"
    assert parse_csv_line(line) == ["", "", ""]

def test_quotes_inside_field_do_not_escape():
    # This implementation toggles in_quotes on every quote, so inner quotes break parsing
    line = '"he said ""hello""",world'
    # According to this code, two consecutive quotes will toggle twice, acting like no quotes
    # Result becomes: he said ""hello"", world
    assert parse_csv_line(line) == ['he said ""hello""', "world"]

def test_no_commas():
    line = "abc"
    assert parse_csv_line(line) == ["abc"]

def test_blank_line():
    line = ""
    assert parse_csv_line(line) == [""]

def test_unbalanced_quotes():
    # The function does not error; it just treats the rest as quoted
    line = '"abc,def'
    assert parse_csv_line(line) == ["abc,def"]

def test_commas_inside_unclosed_quotes():
    line = '"a,b,c'
    assert parse_csv_line(line) == ["a,b,c"]

If you want, I can also add tests checking performance, randomized fuzz tests, or behavior comparisons against Python's csv module.
